Add support for a low-cost 8x8 LED Matrix based on the Max7219 chip as a realtime status display.
Requires 3 signal wires. Some useful debug options are included to demonstrate its usage.

MAX7219_GCODE.name: MAX7219_GCODE
MAX7219_GCODE.comment: Add the M7219 G-code to control the LED matrix

MAX7219_NUMBER_UNITS.name: MAX7219_NUMBER_UNITS
MAX7219_NUMBER_UNITS.comment: Number of Max7219 units in chain.

MAX7219_ROTATE.name: MAX7219_ROTATE
MAX7219_ROTATE.comment: Rotate the display clockwise (in multiples of +/- 90°) connector at:  right=0   bottom=-90  top=90  left=180

MAX7219_REVERSE_ORDER.name: MAX7219_REVERSE_ORDER
MAX7219_REVERSE_ORDER.comment: The order of the LED matrix units may be reversed

MAX7219_REVERSE_EACH.name: MAX7219_REVERSE_EACH
MAX7219_REVERSE_EACH.comment: The LEDs in each matrix unit row may be reversed

MAX7219_SIDE_BY_SIDE.name: MAX7219_SIDE_BY_SIDE
MAX7219_SIDE_BY_SIDE.comment: Big chip+matrix boards can be chained side-by-side

MAX7219_DEBUG_PRINTER_ALIVE.name: MAX7219_DEBUG_PRINTER_ALIVE
MAX7219_DEBUG_PRINTER_ALIVE.comment:
Sample debug features
If you add more debug displays, be careful to avoid conflicts!
MAX7219_DEBUG_PRINTER_ALIVE.notes: Blink corner LED of 8x8 matrix to show that the firmware is functioning

MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_HEAD.name: MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_HEAD
MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_HEAD.comment: Show the planner queue head position on this and the next LED matrix row

MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_TAIL.name: MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_TAIL
MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_TAIL.comment: Show the planner queue tail position on this and the next LED matrix row

MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_QUEUE.name: MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_QUEUE
MAX7219_DEBUG_PLANNER_QUEUE.comment: Show the current planner queue depth on this and the next LED matrix row If you experience stuttering, reboots, etc. this option can reveal how tweaks made to the configuration are affecting the printer in real-time.

MAX7219_DEBUG_PROFILE.name: MAX7219_DEBUG_PROFILE
MAX7219_DEBUG_PROFILE.comment: Display the fraction of CPU time spent in profiled code on this LED matrix row. By default idle() is profiled so this shows how "idle" the processor is. See class CodeProfiler.

MAX7219_DEBUG_MULTISTEPPING.name: MAX7219_DEBUG_MULTISTEPPING
MAX7219_DEBUG_MULTISTEPPING.comment: Show multi-stepping 1 to 128 on this LED matrix row.

MAX7219_DEBUG_SLOWDOWN.name: MAX7219_DEBUG_SLOWDOWN
MAX7219_DEBUG_SLOWDOWN.comment: Count (mod 16) how many times SLOWDOWN has reduced print speed.

MAX7219_REINIT_ON_POWERUP.name: MAX7219_REINIT_ON_POWERUP
MAX7219_REINIT_ON_POWERUP.comment: Re-initialize MAX7129 when power supply turns on

NANODLP_Z_SYNC.name: NANODLP_Z_SYNC
NANODLP_Z_SYNC.comment:
NanoDLP Sync support

Support for Synchronized Z moves when used with NanoDLP. G0/G1 axis moves will
output a "Z_move_comp" string to enable synchronization with DLP projector exposure.
This feature allows you to use [[WaitForDoneMessage]] instead of M400 commands.

NANODLP_ALL_AXIS.name: NANODLP_ALL_AXIS
NANODLP_ALL_AXIS.comment: Send a "Z_move_comp" report for any axis move (not just Z).

MAC_ADDRESS.name: MAC_ADDRESS
MAC_ADDRESS.comment: Ethernet. Use M552 to enable and set the IP address.
MAC_ADDRESS.notes: A MAC address unique to your network

WIFISUPPORT.name: WIFISUPPORT
WIFISUPPORT.comment: Native ESP32 board with WiFi or add-on ESP32 WiFi-101 module
WIFISUPPORT.notes: Marlin embedded WiFi management. Not needed for simple WiFi serial port.

WEBSUPPORT.name: WEBSUPPORT
WEBSUPPORT.comment:
Extras for an ESP32-based motherboard with WIFISUPPORT
These options don't apply to add-on WiFi modules based on ESP32 WiFi101.
WEBSUPPORT.notes: Start a webserver (which may include auto-discovery) using SPIFFS

WIFI_CUSTOM_COMMAND.name: WIFI_CUSTOM_COMMAND
WIFI_CUSTOM_COMMAND.comment: Accept feature config commands (e.g., WiFi ESP3D) from the host

E_MUX0_PIN.name: E_MUX0_PIN
E_MUX0_PIN.comment:
This option only allows the multiplexer to switch on tool-change.
Additional options to configure custom E moves are pending.

Override the default DIO selector pins here, if needed.
Some pins files may provide defaults for these pins.

MMU_SERIAL_PORT.name: MMU_SERIAL_PORT
MMU_SERIAL_PORT.comment:
Common settings for MMU2/MMU2S/MMU3
Serial port used for communication with MMU2/MMU2S/MMU3.

MMU_RST_PIN.name: MMU_RST_PIN
MMU_RST_PIN.comment: Define this pin to use Hardware Reset for MMU2/MMU2S/MMU3

MMU_MENUS.name: MMU_MENUS
MMU_MENUS.comment: Add an LCD menu for MMU2/MMU2S/MMU3

MMU_DEBUG.name: MMU_DEBUG
MMU_DEBUG.comment: Write debug info to serial output

MMU2_MODE_12V.name: MMU2_MODE_12V
MMU2_MODE_12V.comment: Enable if the MMU2 has 12V stepper motors (MMU2 Firmware 1.0.2 and up)

MMU2_FILAMENTCHANGE_EJECT_FEED.name: MMU2_FILAMENTCHANGE_EJECT_FEED
MMU2_FILAMENTCHANGE_EJECT_FEED.comment:
Settings for filament load / unload from the LCD menu.
This is for Průša MK3-style extruders. Customize for your hardware.

MMU2_FILAMENT_RUNOUT_SCRIPT.name: MMU2_FILAMENT_RUNOUT_SCRIPT
MMU2_FILAMENT_RUNOUT_SCRIPT.comment: G-code to execute when MMU2 F.I.N.D.A. probe detects filament runout

MMU2_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_SEQUENCE.name: MMU2_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_SEQUENCE
MMU2_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_SEQUENCE.comment: MMU2 sequences use mm/min. Not compatible with MMU3, which use mm/sec.

MMU2_C0_RETRY.name: MMU2_C0_RETRY
MMU2_C0_RETRY.comment:
Options pertaining to MMU2S devices
Requires the MK3S extruder with a sensor at the extruder idler, like the MMU2S.
See https://help.prusa3d.com/guide/3b-mk3s-mk2-5s-extruder-upgrade_41560#42048, step 11

MMU2_CAN_LOAD_FEEDRATE.name: MMU2_CAN_LOAD_FEEDRATE
MMU2_CAN_LOAD_FEEDRATE.comment:
This is called after the filament runout sensor is triggered to check if
the filament has been loaded properly by moving the filament back and
forth to see if the filament runout sensor is going to get triggered
again, which should not occur if the filament is properly loaded.

Thus, the MMU2_CAN_LOAD_SEQUENCE should contain some forward and
backward moves. The forward moves should be greater than the backward
moves.

This is useless if your filament runout sensor is way behind the gears.
In that case use {0, MMU2_CAN_LOAD_FEEDRATE}

Adjust MMU2_CAN_LOAD_SEQUENCE according to your setup.

MMU2_CAN_LOAD_RETRACT.name: MMU2_CAN_LOAD_RETRACT
MMU2_CAN_LOAD_RETRACT.comment: (mm) Keep under the distance between Load Sequence values

MMU_IR_UNLOAD_MOVE.name: MMU_IR_UNLOAD_MOVE
MMU_IR_UNLOAD_MOVE.comment: Continue unloading if sensor detects filament after the initial unload move

MMU3_FILAMENT_SENSOR_E_POSITION.name: MMU3_FILAMENT_SENSOR_E_POSITION
MMU3_FILAMENT_SENSOR_E_POSITION.comment:
As discussed with our PrusaSlicer profile specialist
- ToolChange shall not try to push filament into the very tip of the nozzle
to have some space for additional G-code to tune the extruded filament length
in the profile
Beware - this value is used to initialize the MMU logic layer - it will be sent to the MMU upon line up (written into its 8bit register 0x0b)
However - in the G-code we can get a request to set the extra load distance at runtime to something else (M708 A0xb Xsomething).
The printer intercepts such a call and sets its extra load distance to match the new value as well.

_MMU3_VERIFY_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_TWEAK.name: _MMU3_VERIFY_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_TWEAK
_MMU3_VERIFY_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_TWEAK.comment: (mm) Amount to adjust the length for verifying load-to-nozzle

MMU3_RETRY_UNLOAD_TO_FINDA_LENGTH.name: MMU3_RETRY_UNLOAD_TO_FINDA_LENGTH
MMU3_RETRY_UNLOAD_TO_FINDA_LENGTH.comment:
The first thing the MMU does is initialize its axis.
Meanwhile the E-motor will unload 20mm of filament in about 1 second.

_MMU_EXTRUDER_PTFE_LENGTH.name: _MMU_EXTRUDER_PTFE_LENGTH
_MMU_EXTRUDER_PTFE_LENGTH.comment:
After loading a new filament, the printer will extrude this length of filament
then retract to the original position. This is used to check if the filament sensor
reading flickers or filament is jammed.

MMU3_SPOOL_JOIN_CONSUMES_ALL_FILAMENT.name: MMU3_SPOOL_JOIN_CONSUMES_ALL_FILAMENT
MMU3_SPOOL_JOIN_CONSUMES_ALL_FILAMENT.comment:
SpoolJoin Consumes All Filament -- EXPERIMENTAL

SpoolJoin normally triggers when FINDA sensor untriggers while printing.
This is the default behaviour and it doesn't consume all the filament
before triggering a filament change. This leaves some filament in the
current slot and before switching to the next slot it is unloaded.

Enabling this option will trigger the filament change when both FINDA
and Filament Runout Sensor triggers during the print and it allows the
filament in the current slot to be completely consumed before doing the
filament change. But this can cause problems as a little bit of filament
will be left between the extruder gears (thinking that the filament
sensor is triggered through the gears) and the end of the PTFE tube and
can cause filament load issues.

MMU3_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_SEQUENCE.name: MMU3_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_SEQUENCE
MMU3_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_SEQUENCE.value: { _MMU_EXTRUDER_PTFE_LENGTH,      MMM_TO_MMS(810) }, /* (13.5 mm/s) Fast load ahead of heatbreak */ { _MMU_EXTRUDER_HEATBREAK_LENGTH, MMM_TO_MMS(198) }  /* ( 3.3 mm/s) Slow load after heatbreak */
MMU3_LOAD_TO_NOZZLE_SEQUENCE.comment: (13.5 mm/s) Fast load ahead of heatbreak */ { _MMU_EXTRUDER_HEATBREAK_LENGTH, MMM_TO_MMS(198) }  /* ( 3.3 mm/s) Slow load after heatbreak */

MMU2_EXTRUDER_SENSOR.name: MMU2_EXTRUDER_SENSOR
MMU2_EXTRUDER_SENSOR.comment:
#define MMU3_RAMMING_SEQUENCE { 0.2816,  MMM_TO_MMS(1339.0) }, { 0.3051,  MMM_TO_MMS(1451.0) }, { 0.3453,  MMM_TO_MMS(1642.0) }, { 0.3990,  MMM_TO_MMS(1897.0) }, { 0.4761,  MMM_TO_MMS(2264.0) }, { 0.5767,  MMM_TO_MMS(2742.0) }, { 0.5691,  MMM_TO_MMS(3220.0) }, { 0.1081,  MMM_TO_MMS(3220.0) }, { 0.7644,  MMM_TO_MMS(3635.0) }, { 0.8248,  MMM_TO_MMS(3921.0) }, { 0.8483,  MMM_TO_MMS(4033.0) }, { -15.0,   MMM_TO_MMS(6000.0) }, { -24.5,   MMM_TO_MMS(1200.0) }, {  -7.0,   MMM_TO_MMS( 600.0) }, {  -3.5,   MMM_TO_MMS( 360.0) }, {  20.0,   MMM_TO_MMS( 454.0) }, { -20.0,   MMM_TO_MMS( 303.0) }, { -35.0,   MMM_TO_MMS(2000.0) }

#else // MMU2 (not MMU2S)

/**
MMU2 Extruder Sensor

Support for a Průša (or other) IR Sensor to detect filament near the extruder
and make loading more reliable. Suitable for an extruder equipped with a filament
sensor less than 38mm from the gears.

During loading the extruder will stop when the sensor is triggered, then do a last
move up to the gears. If no filament is detected, the MMU2 can make some more attempts.
If all attempts fail, a filament runout will be triggered.

MMU2_LOADING_ATTEMPTS_NR.name: MMU2_LOADING_ATTEMPTS_NR
MMU2_LOADING_ATTEMPTS_NR.comment: Number of times to try loading filament before failure

SERVICE_NAME_1.name: SERVICE_NAME_1
SERVICE_NAME_1.comment: Activate up to 3 service interval watchdogs
